1→# CLAUDE.MD -- Academic Project Development with Claude Code
     2→
     3→<!-- HOW TO USE: Replace [BRACKETED PLACEHOLDERS] with your project info.
     4→     Customize Beamer environments and CSS classes for your theme.
     5→     Keep this file under ~150 lines — Claude loads it every session.
     6→     See the guide at docs/workflow-guide.html for full documentation. -->
     7→
     8→**Project:** [YOUR PROJECT NAME]
     9→**Institution:** [YOUR INSTITUTION]
    10→**Branch:** main
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Core Principles
    15→
    16→- **Plan first** -- enter plan mode before non-trivial tasks; save plans to `quality_reports/plans/`
    17→- **Verify after** -- compile/render and confirm output at the end of every task
    18→- **Single source of truth** -- Beamer `.tex` is authoritative; Quarto `.qmd` derives from it
    19→- **Quality gates** -- nothing ships below 80/100
    20→- **[LEARN] tags** -- when corrected, save `[LEARN:category] wrong → right` to MEMORY.md
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## Folder Structure
    25→
    26→```
    27→[YOUR-PROJECT]/
    28→├── CLAUDE.MD                    # This file
    29→├── .claude/                     # Rules, skills, agents, hooks
    30→├── Bibliography_base.bib        # Centralized bibliography
    31→├── Figures/                     # Figures and images
    32→├── Preambles/header.tex         # LaTeX headers
    33→├── Slides/                      # Beamer .tex files
    34→├── Quarto/                      # RevealJS .qmd files + theme
    35→├── docs/                        # GitHub Pages (auto-generated)
    36→├── scripts/                     # Utility scripts + R code
    37→├── quality_reports/             # Plans, session logs, merge reports
    38→├── explorations/                # Research sandbox (see rules)
    39→├── templates/                   # Session log, quality report templates
    40→└── master_supporting_docs/      # Papers and existing slides
    41→```
    42→
    43→---
    44→
    45→## Commands
    46→
    47→```bash
    48→# LaTeX (3-pass, XeLaTeX only)
    49→cd Slides && TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
    50→BIBINPUTS=..:$BIBINPUTS bibtex file
    51→TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
    52→TEXINPUTS=../Preambles:$TEXINPUTS xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode file.tex
    53→
    54→# Deploy Quarto to GitHub Pages
    55→./scripts/sync_to_docs.sh LectureN
    56→
    57→# Quality score
    58→python scripts/quality_score.py Quarto/file.qmd
    59→```
    60→
    61→---
    62→
    63→## Quality Thresholds
    64→
    65→| Score | Gate | Meaning |
    66→|-------|------|---------|
    67→| 80 | Commit | Good enough to save |
    68→| 90 | PR | Ready for deployment |
    69→| 95 | Excellence | Aspirational |
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## Skills Quick Reference
    74→
    75→| Command | What It Does |
    76→|---------|-------------|
    77→| `/compile-latex [file]` | 3-pass XeLaTeX + bibtex |
    78→| `/deploy [LectureN]` | Render Quarto + sync to docs/ |
    79→| `/extract-tikz [LectureN]` | TikZ → PDF → SVG |
    80→| `/proofread [file]` | Grammar/typo/overflow review |
    81→| `/visual-audit [file]` | Slide layout audit |
    82→| `/pedagogy-review [file]` | Narrative, notation, pacing review |
    83→| `/review-r [file]` | R code quality review |
    84→| `/qa-quarto [LectureN]` | Adversarial Quarto vs Beamer QA |
    85→| `/slide-excellence [file]` | Combined multi-agent review |
    86→| `/translate-to-quarto [file]` | Beamer → Quarto translation |
    87→| `/validate-bib` | Cross-reference citations |
    88→| `/devils-advocate` | Challenge slide design |
    89→| `/create-lecture` | Full lecture creation |
    90→| `/commit [msg]` | Stage, commit, PR, merge |
    91→| `/lit-review [topic]` | Literature search + synthesis |
    92→| `/research-ideation [topic]` | Research questions + strategies |
    93→| `/interview-me [topic]` | Interactive research interview |
    94→| `/review-paper [file]` | Manuscript review |
    95→| `/data-analysis [dataset]` | End-to-end R analysis |
    96→

1→---
     2→paths:
     3→  - "master_supporting_docs/**"
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Robust PDF Processing
     7→
     8→## The Safe Processing Workflow
     9→
    10→**Step 1: Receive PDF Upload**
    11→- User uploads PDF to `master_supporting_docs/supporting_papers/` or `supporting_slides/`
    12→- Claude DOES NOT attempt to read it directly
    13→
    14→**Step 2: Check PDF Properties**
    15→```bash
    16→pdfinfo paper_name.pdf | grep "Pages:"
    17→ls -lh paper_name.pdf
    18→```
    19→
    20→**Step 3: Create Subfolder and Split**
    21→```bash
    22→mkdir -p paper_name/
    23→
    24→for i in {0..9}; do
    25→  start=$((i*5 + 1))
    26→  end=$(((i+1)*5))
    27→  gs -sDEVICE=pdfwrite -dNOPAUSE -dBATCH -dSAFER \
    28→     -dFirstPage=$start -dLastPage=$end \
    29→     -sOutputFile="paper_name/paper_name_p$(printf '%03d' $start)-$(printf '%03d' $end).pdf" \
    30→     paper_name.pdf 2>/dev/null
    31→done
    32→```
    33→
    34→**Step 4: Process Chunks Intelligently**
    35→- Read chunks ONE AT A TIME using the Read tool
    36→- Extract key information from each chunk
    37→- Build understanding progressively
    38→- Don't try to hold all chunks in working memory
    39→
    40→**Step 5: Selective Deep Reading**
    41→- After scanning all chunks, identify the most relevant sections
    42→- Only read those sections in detail for slide development
    43→- Skip appendices, references, or less relevant sections unless needed
    44→
    45→## Error Handling Protocol
    46→
    47→**If a chunk fails to process:**
    48→1. Note the problematic chunk (e.g., "Chunk p021-025 failed")
    49→2. Try splitting into 1-2 page pieces
    50→3. If still failing, skip and document the gap
    51→
    52→**If splitting fails:**
    53→1. Check if Ghostscript is installed: `gs --version`
    54→2. Try alternative: `pdftk paper.pdf burst output paper_%03d.pdf`
    55→3. If all else fails, ask user to upload specific page ranges manually
    56→
    57→**If memory/token issues persist:**
    58→1. Process only 2-3 chunks per session
    59→2. Focus on specific sections user identifies as most important
    60→
    61→

content.content:
1→---
     2→name: lit-review
     3→description: Structured literature search and synthesis with citation extraction and gap identification
     4→disable-model-invocation: true
     5→argument-hint: "[topic, paper title, or research question]"
     6→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Write", "WebSearch", "WebFetch"]
     7→---
     8→
     9→# Literature Review
    10→
    11→Conduct a structured literature search and synthesis on the given topic.
    12→
    13→**Input:** `$ARGUMENTS` — a topic, paper title, research question, or phenomenon to investigate.
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## Steps
    18→
    19→1. **Parse the topic** from `$ARGUMENTS`. If a specific paper is named, use it as the anchor.
    20→
    21→2. **Search for related work** using available tools:
    22→   - Check `master_supporting_docs/supporting_papers/` for uploaded papers
    23→   - Use `WebSearch` to find recent publications (if available)
    24→   - Use `WebFetch` to access working paper repositories (if available)
    25→   - Read any existing `.bib` file for papers already in the project
    26→
    27→3. **Organize findings** into these categories:
    28→   - **Theoretical contributions** — models, frameworks, mechanisms
    29→   - **Empirical findings** — key results, effect sizes, data sources
    30→   - **Methodological innovations** — new estimators, identification strategies, inference methods
    31→   - **Open debates** — unresolved disagreements in the literature
    32→
    33→4. **Identify gaps and opportunities:**
    34→   - What questions remain unanswered?
    35→   - What data or methods could address them?
    36→   - Where do findings conflict?
    37→
    38→5. **Extract citations** in BibTeX format for all papers discussed.
    39→
    40→6. **Save the report** to `quality_reports/lit_review_[sanitized_topic].md`
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## Output Format
    45→
    46→```markdown
    47→# Literature Review: [Topic]
    48→
    49→**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
    50→**Query:** [Original query from user]
    51→
    52→## Summary
    53→
    54→[2-3 paragraph overview of the state of the literature]
    55→
    56→## Key Papers
    57→
    58→### [Author (Year)] — [Short Title]
    59→- **Main contribution:** [1-2 sentences]
    60→- **Method:** [Identification strategy / data]
    61→- **Key finding:** [Result with effect size if available]
    62→- **Relevance:** [Why it matters for our research]
    63→
    64→[Repeat for 5-15 papers, ordered by relevance]
    65→
    66→## Thematic Organization
    67→
    68→### Theoretical Contributions
    69→[Grouped discussion]
    70→
    71→### Empirical Findings
    72→[Grouped discussion with comparison across studies]
    73→
    74→### Methodological Innovations
    75→[Methods relevant to the topic]
    76→
    77→## Gaps and Opportunities
    78→
    79→1. [Gap 1 — what's missing and why it matters]
    80→2. [Gap 2]
    81→3. [Gap 3]
    82→
    83→## Suggested Next Steps
    84→
    85→- [Concrete actions: papers to read, data to obtain, methods to consider]
    86→
    87→## BibTeX Entries
    88→
    89→```bibtex
    90→@article{...}
    91→```
    92→```
    93→
    94→---
    95→
    96→## Important
    97→
    98→- **Be honest about uncertainty.** If you cannot verify a citation, say so.
    99→- **Prioritize recent work** (last 5-10 years) unless seminal papers are older.
   100→- **Note working papers vs published papers** — working papers may change.
   101→- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** If you're unsure about a paper's details, flag it for the user to verify.
   102→

<system-reminder>

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→---
     2→name: review-paper
     3→description: Comprehensive manuscript review covering argument structure, econometric specification, citation completeness, and potential referee objections
     4→disable-model-invocation: true
     5→argument-hint: "[paper filename in master_supporting_docs/ or path to .tex/.pdf]"
     6→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Write", "Task"]
     7→---
     8→
     9→# Manuscript Review
    10→
    11→Produce a thorough, constructive review of an academic manuscript — the kind of report a top-journal referee would write.
    12→
    13→**Input:** `$ARGUMENTS` — path to a paper (.tex, .pdf, or .qmd), or a filename in `master_supporting_docs/`.
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## Steps
    18→
    19→1. **Locate and read the manuscript.** Check:
    20→   - Direct path from `$ARGUMENTS`
    21→   - `master_supporting_docs/supporting_papers/$ARGUMENTS`
    22→   - Glob for partial matches
    23→
    24→2. **Read the full paper** end-to-end. For long PDFs, read in chunks (5 pages at a time).
    25→
    26→3. **Evaluate across 6 dimensions** (see below).
    27→
    28→4. **Generate 3-5 "referee objections"** — the tough questions a top referee would ask.
    29→
    30→5. **Produce the review report.**
    31→
    32→6. **Save to** `quality_reports/paper_review_[sanitized_name].md`
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## Review Dimensions
    37→
    38→### 1. Argument Structure
    39→- Is the research question clearly stated?
    40→- Does the introduction motivate the question effectively?
    41→- Is the logical flow sound (question → method → results → conclusion)?
    42→- Are the conclusions supported by the evidence?
    43→- Are limitations acknowledged?
    44→
    45→### 2. Identification Strategy
    46→- Is the causal claim credible?
    47→- What are the key identifying assumptions? Are they stated explicitly?
    48→- Are there threats to identification (omitted variables, reverse causality, measurement error)?
    49→- Are robustness checks adequate?
    50→- Is the estimator appropriate for the research design?
    51→
    52→### 3. Econometric Specification
    53→- Correct standard errors (clustered? robust? bootstrap?)?
    54→- Appropriate functional form?
    55→- Sample selection issues?
    56→- Multiple testing concerns?
    57→- Are point estimates economically meaningful (not just statistically significant)?
    58→
    59→### 4. Literature Positioning
    60→- Are the key papers cited?
    61→- Is prior work characterized accurately?
    62→- Is the contribution clearly differentiated from existing work?
    63→- Any missing citations that a referee would flag?
    64→
    65→### 5. Writing Quality
    66→- Clarity and concision
    67→- Academic tone
    68→- Consistent notation throughout
    69→- Abstract effectively summarizes the paper
    70→- Tables and figures are self-contained (clear labels, notes, sources)
    71→
    72→### 6. Presentation
    73→- Are tables and figures well-designed?
    74→- Is notation consistent throughout?
    75→- Are there any typos, grammatical errors, or formatting issues?
    76→- Is the paper the right length for the contribution?
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Output Format
    81→
    82→```markdown
    83→# Manuscript Review: [Paper Title]
    84→
    85→**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
    86→**Reviewer:** review-paper skill
    87→**File:** [path to manuscript]
    88→
    89→## Summary Assessment
    90→
    91→**Overall recommendation:** [Strong Accept / Accept / Revise & Resubmit / Reject]
    92→
    93→[2-3 paragraph summary: main contribution, strengths, and key concerns]
    94→
    95→## Strengths
    96→
    97→1. [Strength 1]
    98→2. [Strength 2]
    99→3. [Strength 3]
   100→
   101→## Major Concerns
   102→
   103→### MC1: [Title]
   104→- **Dimension:** [Identification / Econometrics / Argument / Literature / Writing / Presentation]
   105→- **Issue:** [Specific description]
   106→- **Suggestion:** [How to address it]
   107→- **Location:** [Section/page/table if applicable]
   108→
   109→[Repeat for each major concern]
   110→
   111→## Minor Concerns
   112→
   113→### mc1: [Title]
   114→- **Issue:** [Description]
   115→- **Suggestion:** [Fix]
   116→
   117→[Repeat]
   118→
   119→## Referee Objections
   120→
   121→These are the tough questions a top referee would likely raise:
   122→
   123→### RO1: [Question]
   124→**Why it matters:** [Why this could be fatal]
   125→**How to address it:** [Suggested response or additional analysis]
   126→
   127→[Repeat for 3-5 objections]
   128→
   129→## Specific Comments
   130→
   131→[Line-by-line or section-by-section comments, if any]
   132→
   133→## Summary Statistics
   134→
   135→| Dimension | Rating (1-5) |
   136→|-----------|-------------|
   137→| Argument Structure | [N] |
   138→| Identification | [N] |
   139→| Econometrics | [N] |
   140→| Literature | [N] |
   141→| Writing | [N] |
   142→| Presentation | [N] |
   143→| **Overall** | **[N]** |
   144→```
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## Principles
   149→
   150→- **Be constructive.** Every criticism should come with a suggestion.
   151→- **Be specific.** Reference exact sections, equations, tables.
   152→- **Think like a referee at a top-5 journal.** What would make them reject?
   153→- **Distinguish fatal flaws from minor issues.** Not everything is equally important.
   154→- **Acknowledge what's done well.** Good research deserves recognition.
   155→- **Do NOT fabricate details.** If you can't read a section clearly, say so.
   156→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1. **PDF location (outside repository):**
   - `/Users/my/Desktop/RA/LeiLei/2024 - Municipal finance shapes urban climate action and justice.pdf`
   - This is the only instance of the requested PDF on the machine; it resides on the Desktop and is not part of the repository, so follow the repository’s PDF handling rules before importing or processing it.

2. **Applicable processing guidance:**
   - `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/rules/pdf-processing.md` – outlines the safe workflow for handling large PDFs (upload destinations, chunk splitting, reflection of errors, and chunk-wise reading with the Read tool). This is directly relevant to analyzing any uploaded PDF, including the municipal finance paper.
   - `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/CLAUDE.md` – contains the overarching project principles (plan-first, verification, folder structure, quality thresholds, and available agent/skill commands) that must guide any work derived from the PDF.
   - `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/rules/plan-first-workflow.md` and `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/rules/meta-governance.md` – stipulate the requirement to plan before non-trivial tasks, reference when to log/session record, and remind that this repo doubles as a template, so keep generic versus specific learnings separated (important when producing derived documents about the PDF).

3. **Helpful skills for analyzing the PDF:**
   - `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/skills/lit-review/SKILL.md` – defines a structured literature-review workflow (including summarizing contributions, thematic organization, and BibTeX extraction), which would be useful if the municipal finance paper is the focus of a literature review.
   - `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/.claude/skills/review-paper/SKILL.md` – provides the six-dimension referee-style review process (argument, identification, econometrics, literature, writing, presentation) plus referees’ objections, ideal for critical analysis of the paper once it’s read.

4. **Supporting directories and scripts:**
   - `quality_reports/` – destination for any plans, reviews, or reports derived from analyzing the PDF.
   - `scripts/` (e.g., `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/scripts/quality_score.py`, `/Users/my/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa8ff484/.Trash/my-project 19.06.43/scripts/sync_to_docs.sh`) – provide utilities for scoring slides or syncing outputs; relevant if the PDF analysis leads to new deliverables.
   - `.claude/skills/` – contains reusable protocols (beyond lit-review/review-paper) for later stages (proofreading, validation, etc.), ensuring any derivative work follows established workflows.

No mention of the PDF exists elsewhere in the repository, so there are no pre-existing notes or scripts specifically tied to that file. The above files outline the procedures and governance that should guide any analysis or derived deliverables for the municipal finance PDF.
